data.title: ICE Canada Weekly Outlook: U.S. Biodiesel Mandate to Be Boon for Canola
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola is poised for more gains as the trade awaits the Trump administration's decision on the latest biodiesel mandate, said Jamie Wilton, broker with RJ O'Brien in Winnipeg.

Wilton said the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency sent its recommendations and a decision on them has yet to come from the White House. He said it's unclear at the moment how Canadian canola could play into that biodiesel mandate. If it's included, then it's a matter of volume and how much its tax credit will be.

The EPA proposed biomass-based diesel at 5.61 billion gallons, a jump of 67.5% from 2025 levels.

"It's a pile of demand that's coming down the line," Wilton said, noting that Chicago soyoil has already been on the rise for some time.

Even if the Trump administration excluded Canadian canola, Wilton stressed there will still be an upside for it.

"Burning beanoil in the fuel tank, canola is going to pick up demand on the food side," he said.

Wilton said the impact from the White House on U.S. soybeans could mirror that of corn when ethanol production was increased.

"You're ramping up biodiesel production, so you will need the feedstocks to do that," he said.

As for the Statistics Canada forecast on planted area in 2026/27, Wilton suggested the report likely won't have much of an impact on the canola market the day it's released.

The trade widely expects planted canola acres to increase from 21.62 million seeded in 2025/26. Some in the market pegged the number at 22.50 million acres, cautioning yields are likely to drop to normal levels in 2026/27.

"We'll need every stitch of canola we can produce," Wilton said.

He added, the Canadian Grain Commission should be soon showing increases in canola exports in its weekly reports. The destinations will be confirmed in the CGC's monthly reports, with the trade looking for a major increase of canola to China.

In the meantime, with the latest Middle East war, Wilton said it's difficult to see where the commodities will go "from minute to minute and hour by hour."

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com

data.title: AST SpaceMobile's Plan to Beat SpaceX's Starlink. What's Propelling the Stock. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

AST SpaceMobile was rising sharply on Wednesday. The company is bouncing back from a recent dip as it got a new investor and prepares to compete with Elon Musk's SpaceX in the field of satellite-to-cell communications.

AST SpaceMobile was rising 13% to $105.11 in early trading. That came after it announced Canadian communications firm Telus Corp. as a new partner and equity shareholder in the company late on Tuesday.

The stock has more than tripled in the 12 months but is notably volatile, having plunged from highs of more than $120 earlier this year to lows of less than $80 following a recent convertible debt issue.

AST SpaceMobile is racing to get enough satellites into space in order to deliver 5G-quality voice, data, and video coverage to cellphones anywhere in the world. In addition to Telus, it has agreements with dozens of major mobile network operators including AT&T and Verizon.

However, it is facing off against a major rival in the form of Musk's SpaceX. This week, SpaceX confirmed the rebranding of its own direct-to-device offering as "Starlink Mobile" in a presentation at the Mobile World Congress (MWC) telecoms conference in Barcelona. Accompanying the announcement, SpaceX said its next-generation satellites will deliver 5G speeds from space, with 100 times the data density of its current hardware.

Notably, Starlink Mobile is set to offer download speeds up to 150 megabits per second in ideal conditions, according to a presentation made at MWC by Michael Nicolls, SpaceX's senior vice president of Starlink engineering, on Monday.

That throws the gauntlet down to AST SpaceMobile, which has long suggested that its own satellites — which are much larger but fewer in number than the thousands of Starlink craft — will offer superior performance. AST SpaceMobile has said its own latest satellite is capable of exceeding peak data speeds of 120 megabits per second.

AST SpaceMobile President Scott Wisniewski told Barron's that he wasn't worried about Starlink Mobile's claims.

"In terms of stated speeds we'll let the live demonstrations speak for themselves," Wisniewski said, in an interview at MWC. "At the end of the day it's driven by how much spectrum you have...and the size of your satellite, and we're flying the largest ever communications array put into low Earth orbit."

Another key determining factor is likely to be whether AST SpaceMobile can get its satellites into orbit quickly enough to establish a commercial foothold before its giant rival catches up. With six satellites currently in orbit, it needs between 45 and 60 operational to offer a commercial service in northern latitudes, a goal it is aiming to achieve this year across 12 contracted rocket launches.

If all goes according to plan, that would mean AST SpaceMobile is operating in key markets ahead of its rival. SpaceX's Nicholls said that his company plans to launch its direct-to-cell capable satellites in mid-2027 and can put up a constellation capable of "global and contiguous coverage" within six months.

AST SpaceMobile's army of fervent retail trader fans will be hoping that by then, their own company's satellites are already tracing their own celestial path.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Stuart Rush Launches as an Independent Industrial Manufacturing Company Following Divestiture from Columbus McKinnon
data.description:
DAMASCUS, VA / ACCESS Newswire / March 4, 2026 / Stuart Rush today announced its official launch as a standalone industrial manufacturing company following its divestiture from Columbus McKinnon.

While the company name is new, the products, experienced teams, and long-standing distributor relationships remain in place. Stuart Rush will continue serving distributors and end users across industrial manufacturing and material handling applications.

"Our distributor partners and customers know this business, and they know our team," said Spencer Darr, President/CCO of Stuart Rush. "As we move forward as an independent company, our commitment to product quality, clear communication, and long-term relationships remains unchanged."

Stuart Rush manufactures industrial lifting and material handling products designed for reliable performance in demanding applications.

For more information, visit www.stuart-rush.com

Media Contacts:

Anthony Alessi

Director of Marketing & Analytics

Marketing@stuart-rush.com

Vicki Nazzaro

Customer Experience & Strategic Programs Manager

Marketing@stuart-rush.com

SOURCE: Stuart Rush

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire
